exemplar.title:
How does income, education level, and age affect infectious disease
epidemic preparedness

detailedExemplarData.title:
How does income, education level, and age affect infectious disease
epidemic preparedness

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Topic and purpose are clearly communicated with explicit spatial framing of Metro Vancouver
Research question is stated clearly and methodology is robust, combining primary and secondary sources
Methodology section includes mapping of survey points and description of chi-square testing, demonstrating replicability
weaknesses: Minor phrasing lapses (unfinished hypothesis sentence) slightly blur precision
Research question split across two lines disrupts clarity
Sample size (n = 51) is not explicitly stated in the methodology section
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates accurate and effective communication of topic, a focused research question and a complete, appropriate methodology. Minor lapses in phrasing and presentation of the question prevent a full-mark award, placing this criterion solidly at 5 of 6.

strengths: Geographical and epidemiological knowledge is clear, supported by census data and peer-reviewed literature
Terminology (e.g. âconvenience sampling,â âchi-square statistic,â âspatial inequalityâ) is used accurately and consistently
Rationale for selecting socio-economic factors demonstrates strong subject understanding
weaknesses: In-text citations lack full author-date details and some DOI/publication details are missing
Calculation of degrees of freedom is briefly misdescribed
Expected frequencies are not shown before the ÏÂ² calculation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge is clear and appropriately supported by sources, and technical vocabulary is applied consistently. Small citation and calculation oversights mean the work does not reach the topmost level of excellence, so 5 of 6 is warranted.

strengths: Primary data are applied directly to statistical tests, with secondary literature effectively informing interpretation
Conclusions acknowledge sampling bias and limitations, demonstrating critical awareness
Argument is logically structured around the three factors and returns to hypotheses in a balanced conclusion
weaknesses: P-values and effect sizes (e.g. Cramerâs V) are not reported to strengthen statistical inferences
Discussion lacks deeper critique of sample representativeness and statistical power considerations
Spatial autocorrelation and alternative analytical approaches are not discussed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay shows good to very good performance across research application, analysis and argument, with well-supported conclusions and coherent structure. Occasional gaps in advanced evaluation place it at the upper end of the 7-9 band, meriting a 9 of 12.

strengths: Structure is clear and complete, with a formal table of contents and logical section flow
Headings, sub-headings, tables and figures are neatly presented and consistent
Layout considerations (font, spacing) are correctly applied throughout
weaknesses: Maps lack north arrows and scale bars, and some figure captions miss source citations
Page numbers are implied by the TOC but not visible in the extract
Reference list entries occasionally omit DOIs or full publication details
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reportâs organization and overall layout strongly support reader comprehension, meeting the top band for structure and layout. Minor cartographic and citation refinements are needed but do not materially impede understanding, justifying a 4 of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Primary data are applied directly to the chi-square tests and linked to each socio-economic factor; secondary literature is marshalled to interpret results.  Relevance is generally clear, but occasional digressions (lengthy theoretical narratives only loosely tied to data) prevent the consistently sophisticated linking required for the upper two bands, placing the work solidly in the 4-6 range.

A primary questionnaire, convenience-sample strategy, mapping of survey points and chi-square statistical test are all described.  Secondary sources (census, WHO data, academic papers) are listed and later cited.  Limitations of the sampling method are acknowledged.  Although the sample is small (n = 51) and convenience-based, the range of methods is appropriate for the stated aim, satisfying the âcomplete with appropriate rangeâ description.

The student explains socio-economic determinants of health, quotes chi-square calculations and refers to numerous peer-reviewed studies and census data, demonstrating clear (though not exceptional) geographical and epidemiological knowledge.  Integration of theory such as spatial inequality is present but not deeply developed, so the work sits comfortably in the âclear knowledgeâ band rather than âexcellentâ.

Headings, sub-headings, tables and figures are neatly presented; font and spacing are consistent.  Page numbers are implied by the TOC but not visible in the extract; figure captions lack full source referencing.  These minor flaws justify a secure but not perfect score within the top band.

Terms including âconvenience samplingâ, âsignificance level 0.05â, âdegrees of freedomâ, âchi-square statisticâ, âhealth-literacyâ and âspatial inequalityâ are used accurately and consistently throughout the essay.  Misuse of specialist vocabulary is rare, justifying a high score within the top band.

The essayâs title, abstracted purpose and research focus are stated in the opening page, and the research question is reiterated in the introduction.  The geographical location (Metro Vancouver) is mapped and visually highlighted, so the spatial frame is explicit.  Vocabulary such as âinfectious-disease epidemic preparednessâ is used consistently, leaving little doubt about what is being investigated.  Minor lapses in phrasing (e.g., an unfinished sentence in the introduction) slightly blur precision, so a mid-high rather than full-mark judgement is appropriate.

The argument follows a logical three-factor structure, returning to the hypotheses and drawing a balanced conclusion.  Paragraphing is generally coherent, yet occasional repetition and some over-long descriptive passages introduce minor inconsistencies.  This aligns with an âeffective argument with minor inconsistenciesâ in the 4-6 band.

The student performs statistical calculations, compares statistics to critical values and draws conclusions.  Limitations of convenience sampling and the non-significant age result are acknowledged, showing some evaluation.  Nonetheless, no confidence intervals or alternative explanations are explored, and critique of data reliability remains surface-level, so the analysis is âmostly supportedâ rather than âwell-supportedâ.

A formal table of contents, numbered sections, introductionâmethodâresultsâdiscussionâconclusion flow and labelled figures provide a clear scaffold that aids reader comprehension.  All core EE elements are present and ordered logically, meriting the upper band for structure.

The research question is clearly quoted and centred on three specific independent variables, giving it recognisable focus.  Nonetheless, treating income, education and age simultaneously broadens the task and risks superficial coverage, indicating that the question is "clearly stated but only partially focused" rather than fully optimised for depth.

annotations.comment: The table of contents is well structured and clearly guides the reader through the sections.
The student has justified the use of primary and secondary sources and mapped survey points, demonstrating a robust methodology.
Figure 2 is informative but should include a north arrow and scale bar to meet cartographic standards.
The research question is split across two lines, which disrupts clarity. Consolidate it into one continuous statement.
The title is clear and accurately conveys the investigationâs focus, aiding reader orientation.
The student has correctly provided a word count at the start, fulfilling formatting requirements.
The introduction describes COVID-19 broadly but does not explicitly integrate the research question. Rephrase to connect background to your specific focus.
Citations such as â(WHO)â lack precise reference details. Ensure all in-text citations follow a consistent style with author and date.
Figure 1 effectively locates the study area but omits a scale bar and north arrow. Add these map elements to enhance spatial clarity.
The hypothesis paragraph ends abruptly mid-sentence, which disrupts clarity. Complete the thought to maintain precision.
The methodology section explains methods well but omits the sample size. State explicitly that n = 51 in this section.
The inclusion of sample survey questions improves transparency and replicability of the research design.
The scoring rubric for preparedness is not justified. Explain why point values and weighting were chosen.
The student acknowledges convenience sampling bias and outlines mitigation efforts, showing critical awareness of limitations.
Figure 4 is clearly labelled but omits a source citation. Provide the origin of the data or creation date.
The chi-square distribution table is cited to Scribbr but lacks full reference details. Include author and retrieval date.
The frequency table for age is clearly formatted; consider adding expected frequencies for completeness before ÏÂ² calculation.
The conclusion reflects on key findings and limitations, demonstrating analytical reflection on the studyâs scope.
The description of degrees of freedom reverses rows and columns; verify that df = (number of rows â 1)Ã(number of columns â 1)
Expected frequencies must be shown before calculating ÏÂ². Include a worked example of these calculations.
Statistical results lack p-values. Report p-values alongside ÏÂ² to strengthen inference on significance.
The frequency table for education is well presented, but consider adding an effect size or Cramerâs V to quantify association strength.
The discussion of education results is descriptive but lacks critique of potential data limitations. Reflect on sample representativeness.
The integration of peer-reviewed literature on education strengthens the argument by contextualizing your findings.
The rationale for exploring age as a factor demonstrates strong subject knowledge of demographic influences.
After reporting a non-significant age result, discuss statistical power given the small sample size to assess risk of Type II error.
References are generally consistent but several entries lack DOI or publication details. Verify each citation for completeness.
The reflection on future research and methodological improvements is brief; expand this evaluative component.
The recognition of sampling assumptions about Vancouver residents shows honest critical engagement.
Consider discussing spatial autocorrelation in neighborhood preparedness metrics to address potential clustering effects.
